did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses a computational approach involving an anisotropic diffusion filter, an active contour model without edges for segmentation, and a Support Vector Machine (SVM) for the classification of pigmented skin lesions in macroscopic images to assist in skin cancer diagnosis.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents a novel computational approach for detecting pigmented skin lesions, detailing specific methods (anisotropic diffusion filter, active contour model, SVM) and reporting experimental results regarding segmentation and classification. It is an original study published in Expert Systems With Applications, not a review or survey.

title: A computational approach for detecting pigmented skin lesions in macroscopic images
brief_summary: This study presents a computational approach for detecting pigmented skin lesions in macroscopic images to assist in the early diagnosis of skin cancer. The method utilizes an anisotropic diffusion filter for pre-processing, an active contour model without edges for segmentation, and a Support Vector Machine (SVM) for classification based on asymmetry, border, color, and texture features. Experiments were conducted on macroscopic images to evaluate the segmentation and classification performance of the proposed system. The results obtained for the segmentation and classification of pigmented skin lesions were reported as very promising. This work contributes to the development of automated tools for dermatological analysis by addressing challenges such as inconsistent imaging conditions and artifacts.

all_task_types.target: Skin lesion types (benign vs. malignant, specifically melanoma, seborrheic keratosis, and nevus)
all_task_types.details: The primary task is the classification of pigmented skin lesions into diagnostic categories (benign or malignant) using a Support Vector Machine (SVM) based on extracted features such as asymmetry, border, color, and texture. The abstract and introduction explicitly state the goal is to 'diagnose skin lesion types' and perform 'classification'.

However, the provided text chunks do not specify the source of these images (e.g., whether it is a public dataset like ISIC, a private hospital dataset, or self-collected), nor do they provide specific counts or splits.

No specific quantitative metrics (e.g., accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, Dice coefficient) or numerical values are reported in the provided text context.

components: Active contour model without edges (for segmentation)
architecture_details.feature_extraction: Asymmetry, border, colour, and texture analysis

preprocessing_techniques: Anisotropic diffusion filter (used for image pre-processing to handle inconsistent imaging conditions, poor resolution, and artifacts such as hair, reflections, shadows, and skin lines)
conclusions: The proposed computational approach, utilizing an anisotropic diffusion filter, an active contour model without edges, and a Support Vector Machine (SVM), is effective for extracting skin lesion features based on asymmetry, border, color, and texture analysis to diagnose skin lesion types.

The method successfully addresses challenges in computational analysis of skin lesion images, such as inconsistent imaging conditions, poor resolution, and artifacts like hair, reflections, shadows, and skin lines, by employing pre-processing and segmentation techniques.

Experiments conducted on macroscopic images demonstrated that the results for the segmentation and classification of pigmented skin lesions were very promising, indicating the potential of the system to assist dermatologists in the early diagnosis of skin cancer.
important_remarks: The paper does not report specific quantitative metrics (e.g., accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, Dice coefficient) or numerical values for segmentation and classification performance, describing results only as 'very promising'.

The source of the macroscopic images used for experiments is not specified in the provided context (e.g., public dataset vs. private collection), and no details on dataset size, class distribution, or train/test splits are provided.
No statistical methods, hypothesis testing, p-values, or confidence intervals are reported to support the claim of 'very promising' results.

The study focuses on macroscopic (clinical) images, which are noted to have inconsistent imaging conditions (variable distances, illumination), poor resolution, and artifacts (hair, reflections, shadows, skin lines) that hinder analysis.

The methodology relies on traditional machine learning (SVM) with hand-crafted features (asymmetry, border, color, texture) rather than deep learning, which may limit generalizability compared to modern approaches.

The paper addresses the clinical challenge of distinguishing melanoma from benign lesions (seborrheic keratosis, nevus) which can resemble melanoma in initial states, but lacks external validation evidence in the provided text.
